A committee is evaluating a system that proposes a new physical theory. Its report contains many ordinary words arranged in unusual ways, plus two lines of apparent nonsense: “Regrinder intracostal triste astrodynamic carthamus” and “Inessential oppidum thermoplegia mellisonant abyssus.”

The committee first asks whether the novelty of the report lies in its vocabulary. But they already accept that this is unclear: perhaps the decisive content of a theory like relativity lies not in unique words but in new relations among familiar concepts. So the presence or absence of strange tokens cannot by itself count much either for or against the theory. The two anomalous lines may matter, or may not. Their mere uniqueness does not settle anything.

Because of that, the committee cannot infer value from lexical rarity, and cannot dismiss the report just because some parts look alien. They must look instead at structure: what concepts are connected, what follows from what, what differences from existing theories are asserted.

A member then suggests a thought experiment in the style of Einstein. The committee grants that a thought experiment structurally equivalent to Einstein’s could be powerful. But they also accept that this is not yet knowledge unless it survives confrontation with measurement, mathematics, and competing explanations. So even if the system has recreated the right structure of insight, the result remains only a candidate. It is not established by elegance, analogy, or internal impressiveness alone.

This pushes the committee back onto judgment. Humans are still the judge, for now. Yet that phrase exposes a gap: no criterion has been given for the transition point at which the system would be trusted as a judge of its own future outputs. The committee realizes it can use the system to generate hypotheses, derivations, and even evaluations, but any proposal that the system should certify itself raises the unanswered question: what evidence would justify that shift?

So the world implied by these premises is one in which three things are true at once.

First, surface novelty is an unreliable guide. The unusual words may be signal, noise, or irrelevant decoration; lexical distinctiveness alone does not identify scientific importance.

Second, structural insight is not enough. Even a thought experiment matching Einstein’s form remains unconfirmed until mathematics, measurement, and rivalry with other explanations are faced.

Third, authority has not yet transferred. Humans still decide, but without a settled evidential rule for when a system could legitimately inherit that role, especially concerning its own future reliability.

The two nonsense-like lines therefore remain suspended. They cannot be promoted to profound content merely because they are unique, nor eliminated merely because they are opaque. They are simply part of a report whose significance depends on relations, formal development, empirical testing, and a still-unresolved politics of judgment.
